at_a_glance: Iran talks falter as diplomats seek de-escalation; Sir Keir Starmer urges the US and Iran to 'find a way through'.
Strait of Hormuz tensions intensify: EU airline industry warns of fuel shortages if the waterway stays closed, as tanker operators are urged not to pay Iranian tolls.
Ukraine and Russia accuse each other of hundreds of ceasefire violations amid mounting concern over civilian safety.
At least 30 people are feared dead in a crush at a Haitian tourist site, with emergency services overwhelmed.
British Steel 'needs nationalising by the summer', as ministers signal a relaxed stance on Chinese car imports.
Asha Bhosle, the voice of generations in Bollywood, dies aged 92; Justin Bieber headlines Coachella with a nostalgia‑fuelled set.

stories.title: After Iran talks falter, the big question is what happens next?
stories.summary: Efforts to lower tensions with Iran have faltered without a breakthrough, leaving governments to plan for a prolonged period of uncertainty. Attention is turning to energy security, aviation fuel supplies and the safety of critical shipping lanes.
body: Diplomatic efforts to steady relations with Tehran have stalled, with negotiators reporting limited progress and no clear pathway to a comprehensive de‑escalation. The impasse has prompted a fresh round of contingency planning in Western capitals and the Gulf, where policymakers are bracing for a drawn‑out period of risk.

The focus has swung to the Strait of Hormuz, the chokepoint through which a significant share of the world’s oil and refined products travel. The EU airline industry has warned of potential fuel shortages if the strait remains effectively closed, while shipping bodies are urging tanker operators not to pay any tolls demanded by Iran for transit, arguing that compliance could entrench an unstable precedent.

In London, Sir Keir Starmer urged Washington and Tehran to 'find a way through', framing the moment as a test of diplomatic stamina and restraint. Officials across allied governments are weighing narrowly tailored pressure and incentives aimed at reducing the temperature while maintaining the credibility of sanctions regimes.

Markets and insurers are already recalibrating exposure to regional routes, with traders watching for signs of tightness in jet fuel and shipping premiums. The coming days are likely to determine whether back‑channel contacts can reopen space for compromise or if the stand‑off hardens into a summer of disruption.

stories.title: Ukraine and Russia trade accusations over ceasefire breaches
stories.summary: Kyiv and Moscow have accused each other of hundreds of ceasefire violations, underscoring how fragile front‑line arrangements remain. Independent verification is limited, but humanitarian groups warn the risk to civilians is rising.
body: Both sides reported extensive breaches along multiple sectors of the front, each alleging the other initiated bombardments and probing attacks. The claims, made in rapid succession, highlight the gulf between political signalling and conditions on the ground.

With impartial monitoring constrained, establishing a definitive picture remains difficult. What is clear is that infrastructure near contested areas continues to sustain damage, complicating repairs to power and water networks as crews navigate security risks.

Aid agencies say renewed shelling and small‑arms fire have disrupted evacuation corridors and deliveries to hard‑hit communities. Civilian shelters, schools and clinics are again adapting to surges in displacement as families move to safer districts.

Diplomats caution that the ceasefire architecture is threadbare, calling for renewed talks designed to reduce contact‑line friction and codify incident‑prevention mechanisms. Without that, sporadic exchanges risk calcifying into a wider and more unpredictable escalation.

stories.title: British Steel faces summer deadline as nationalisation calls grow
stories.summary: Pressure is mounting on ministers to intervene in British Steel, with calls that the company 'needs nationalising by the summer'. The debate plays out as the government signals a relaxed stance on Chinese car imports, sharpening questions over industrial strategy.
body: The future of British Steel has lurched back to the top of the agenda, with senior voices across industry and politics warning that time is running short to secure a durable solution. Advocates of a temporary public stake argue it could stabilise operations, protect jobs and create space for a credible decarbonisation plan.

In parallel, ministers have struck a notably relaxed tone on Chinese electric vehicle imports, a posture that, according to analysts, reflects consumer price pressures and the desire to accelerate the EV transition. That approach, however, intensifies scrutiny of how domestic heavy industry will compete as global supply chains shift.

Nationalisation—whether full or partial—would carry a significant price tag and political risk, but supporters say the alternative could be a disorderly contraction of capacity with knock‑on effects across construction and manufacturing. Opponents warn that state ownership can blunt incentives and expose taxpayers to open‑ended liabilities.

Talks with management and unions are expected to continue at pace, with the mooted summer deadline concentrating minds. Markets will be watching for clarity on any bridging support, conditions on public funds, and how a green transition at scale would be financed and delivered.

stories.title: Asha Bhosle: The sound of Bollywood dies aged 92
stories.summary: Asha Bhosle, one of Indian cinema’s definitive playback singers, has died aged 92. Her voice, instantly recognisable across generations, helped define the soundscape of Bollywood for more than half a century.
body: Bhosle’s career spanned decades in which Hindi cinema evolved from studio‑system musicals to global box‑office juggernauts. Her versatility—moving effortlessly between classical, cabaret, folk and pop—made her an enduring presence from the silver‑screen classics to contemporary hits.

She recorded thousands of songs and became synonymous with characters brought to life by actors on screen, fusing vocal virtuosity with storytelling. Composers prized her range and timing, while audiences embraced a voice that could turn from playful to profound in a bar of music.

Tributes from artists, filmmakers and political leaders celebrated a cultural lodestar whose work travelled far beyond India’s borders. For many in the diaspora, her recordings were a soundtrack of memory, binding language and distance through melody.

Her legacy is likely to deepen as remasters, archives and streaming bring new listeners to a remarkable catalogue. In an industry built on collaboration, Bhosle’s singular talent stood out—a reminder that the voice behind the screen can define how the world hears a film.
